definition: An occultist studies and practices the hidden arts — the 'occult sciences' of astrology, magic, and spirit-communication pursued as a unified body of secret knowledge. The term is deliberately broad where magus, conjurer, or diviner are specific: an occultist is defined less by a single technique than by a commitment to what lies concealed from ordinary sight.
history: The word builds on Latin *occultus*, 'hidden,' with the agent-suffix *-ist* — but the practitioner-noun is a modern coinage, arriving in the nineteenth century alongside Spiritualism and the revival of ceremonial magic. The medieval and Renaissance materials it retrospectively organizes knew no such job title: they spoke of the *magus*, of *philosophia occulta* — the 'hidden philosophy' Agrippa systematized — and of specific arts. The turn is precisely that Victorian moment when the scattered 'occult sciences' were gathered under one name and one self-conscious identity. The word lands as the title for the modern esotericist.
commentary: The job title is younger than the practice. 'Occultism,' and with it the 'occultist,' is a nineteenth-century coinage, born with Spiritualism and the revival of ceremonial magic, and the field now insists it not be read back onto earlier centuries. Wouter Hanegraaff and Marco Pasi have argued that 'occultism' — and the broader category 'Western esotericism' — are recently constructed scholarly objects: Agrippa spoke of *philosophia occulta*, not of being an occultist. The turn is to treat the noun as a historical artifact of modernity's own polemics (the 'occult' as the rejected, disenchanted remainder) rather than a timeless kind of person.

commentary_sources.label: Wouter Hanegraaff, Peter Forshaw & Marco Pasi (eds.), Hermes Explains
commentary_sources.abstract: This is a collection of short, plain-language answers by leading scholars to thirty basic questions about Western esotericism (a broad label for historical traditions like alchemy, astrology, and magic that mainstream religion and science pushed to the margins), aimed at readers with no prior background in the subject.

commentary_sources.label: Wouter Hanegraaff, Esotericism and the Academy
commentary_sources.abstract: Explains why universities have historically dismissed astrology, alchemy, and magic as "not real knowledge," and argues that this rejection says as much about how scholars built their own identity as it does about the actual content of those traditions.

commentary_sources.label: Cornelius Agrippa, De Occulta Philosophia
commentary_sources.abstract: Written in the early 1500s, this is Renaissance scholar Cornelius Agrippa's sweeping handbook of magic, describing three connected levels of hidden power -- in nature, in the stars, and in the divine -- and explaining how a learned magician could try to tap into each one; this edition is the standard modern scholarly edition of the original Latin text.

etymology.note: A Victorian coinage for a practitioner of the 'occult sciences' — the word arrives exactly when Spiritualism does.
etymology.hook: A job title invented by the Victorians.

mechanics_snippets.html:
Imoen, <mark>Occult</mark> Trickster can't be blocked.
Whenever Imoen deals combat damage to a player, you may exile an instant or sorcery card from your graveyard. If you do, create a 2/2 black Zombie creature token.
